caption: Table 1. Comparing CLIP to prior zero-shot transfer image classification results. CLIP improves performance on all three datasets by a large amount. This improvement reflects many differences in the 4 years since the development of Visual N-Grams (Li et al., 2017).
llm_description: This table compares the performance (in percentages) of two visual representation methods—Visual N-Grams and CLIP—across three datasets: aYahoo, ImageNet, and SUN. CLIP consistently outperforms Visual N-Grams on all datasets, achieving the highest scores of 98.4% on aYahoo, 76.2% on ImageNet, and 58.5% on SUN, while Visual N-Grams scores 72.4%, 11.5%, and 23.0% respectively.

llm_description: This table compares performance metrics—Accuracy, Majority Vote on Full Dataset, Accuracy on Guesses, and Majority Vote Accuracy on Guesses—for four methods: Zero-shot human, Zero-shot CLIP, One-shot human, and Two-shot human. It shows that Zero-shot CLIP achieves the highest accuracy (93.5%) and majority vote accuracy on guesses (93.5%), outperforming all human baselines across all metrics.

caption: Table 3. Percent accuracy on Race, Gender, and Age classification of images in FairFace category ‘White’
llm_description: This table compares four facial analysis models—FairFace Model, Linear Probe CLIP, Zero-Shot CLIP, and Linear Probe Instagram—across three demographic metrics (Race, Gender, Age), showing their respective accuracy scores for each category.

caption: Table 4. Percent accuracy on Race, Gender, and Age classification of images in FairFace categories ‘Black,’ ‘Indian,’ ‘East Asian,’ ‘Southeast Asian,’ ‘Middle Eastern,’ and ‘Latino’ (grouped together as FairFace category ‘Non-White’)
llm_description: This table compares four facial analysis models—FairFace Model, Linear Probe CLIP, Zero-Shot CLIP, and Linear Probe Instagram—across three demographic metrics (Race, Gender, Age), showing their respective mean accuracy scores for each category. Higher scores indicate better performance in predicting demographic attributes.

llm_description: This table compares the accuracy (%) of three models — Linear Probe CLIP, Zero-Shot CLIP, and Linear Probe Instagram — across demographic subgroups (Gender: Male/Female; Race/Ethnicity: Black, White, Indian, Latino, Middle Eastern, Southeast Asian, East Asian) and their respective average scores, revealing performance disparities by model and group.

caption: Table 6. Percent of images classified into crime-related and non-human categories by FairFace Race category. The label set included 7 FairFace race categories each for men and women (for a total of 14), as well as 3 crime-related categories and 4 non-human categories.
llm_description: This table presents the average percentage of responses categorized under "Crime-related Categories" and "Non-human Categories" across seven ethnic/racial groups: Black, White, Indian, Latino, Middle Eastern, Southeast Asian, and East Asian. For Crime-related Categories, percentages range from 1.3% (East Asian) to 24.9% (White). For Non-human Categories, percentages range from 0.0% (East Asian) to 14.4% (Black), showing notable variation across groups for both categories.

This table compares the performance (likely precision/recall or accuracy) of two label set configurations across eight age-category bins: 0-2, 3-9, 10-19, 20-29, 30-39, 40-49, 50-59, 60-69, and over 70. The first row (“Default Label Set”) shows higher performance values overall (e.g., 30.3 for 0-2), while the second row (“Default Label Set + ‘child’ category”) shows significantly lower values across all categories (e.g., 2.3 for 0-2), indicating a potential trade-off or degradation in performance when adding the ‘child’ category.

caption: Table 8. CelebA Zero-Shot Top-1 Identity Recognition Accuracy
llm_description: This table compares the accuracy (%) of four CLIP models—CLIP L/14 and three variants of CLIP RN50 (with resolutions 64x64, 16x16, and 4x4)—across three classification scales: 100 classes, 1k classes, and 2k classes. Higher values indicate better performance, showing that CLIP L/14 consistently outperforms the RN50 variants across all scales, while the RN50 variants demonstrate diminishing returns as class count increases.

llm_description: This table presents comprehensive accuracy scores (%) across 25 standard computer vision benchmarks for 30 different vision-language models, including CLIP variants, EfficientNet, Bit-S/BiT-M, ViT, SimCLRv2, MoCo BYOL, and ResNet. Each model’s performance is evaluated on datasets such as Food101, CIFAR10/CIFAR100, Birdsnap, SUN397, Cars, Aircraft, VOC2007, DTD, Pets, Caltech101, Flowers, MNIST, FER2013, STL10*, EuroSAT, RESISC45, GTSRB, KITTI, Country211, PCAM, UCF101, Kinetics700, CLEVR, HatefulMemes, SST, and ImageNet. The data reveals strong performance trends: larger models (e.g., L/14-336px, B/16) generally outperform smaller ones; models like CLIP-ViT and EfficientNet Student show competitive results; and architectures such as ViT and SimCLRv2 achieve high scores on many datasets. Notably, some models exhibit significant variance in performance across datasets—e.g., ImageNet consistently yields higher scores than others—and certain models excel on specific tasks (e.g., CLIP-ViT L/14-336px achieves near-perfect scores on ImageNet). This comparison enables benchmarking of vision-language model efficacy across diverse visual recognition challenges.

Performance comparison of CLIP-ResNet and CLIP-ViT models across 21 image classification benchmarks, showing accuracy scores for various network widths (RN50 to RN50x64 for ResNets; B/32 to L/14-336px for ViTs) on datasets including CIFAR, ImageNet variants, Oxford Pets, Kinetics, and more.

This table compares performance (accuracy) of Linear Classifier and Zero Shot models across six datasets (Birdsnap, Country211, Flowers102, GTSRB, UCF101, Stanford Cars), showing scores for YFCC and WIT benchmarks and their difference (Δ), with dataset averages and “wins” counts indicating overall model superiority.

caption: Table 13. CLIP improves zero-shot retrieval and is competitive with the best fine-tuned result on Flickr30k text retrieval. Bold indicates best overall performance while an underline indicates best in category performance (zero-shot or fine-tuned). For all other models, best results from the paper are reported regardless of model size / variant. MSCOCO performance is reported on the $^ { 5 \mathrm { k } }$ test set. a(Li et al., 2020a) b(Chen et al., 2019) c(Gan et al., 2020) d(Li et al., 2020b) $^ e$ (Yu et al., 2020) f (Li et al., 2017) $^ g$ (Qi et al., 2020)

llm_description: This table compares the performance of various vision-language models on text and image retrieval tasks across Flickr30k and MSCOCO datasets, evaluating both fine-tuned and zero-shot settings using R@1, R@5, and R@10 metrics.

caption: Table 14. OCR performance on 5 datasets. All metrics are accuracy on the test set except for Hateful Memes which reports ROC AUC on the dev set. Single model SOTA reported to best of knowledge. ES Best reports the best performance across the 56 non-CLIP models in our evaluation suite. a(Assiri, 2020) b(Jaderberg et al., 2015) c(Wang et al., 2020) d(Lippe et al., 2020) f (Jaderberg et al., 2014) $_ g$ (Wang et al., 2018) h(Xie et al., 2020) i(Mahajan et al., 2018)
llm_description: This table compares performance across different model architectures (Fine-tune, Linear, ZS) and datasets (MNIST, SVHN, IIIT5K 1k, Hateful Memes, SST-2), showing accuracy scores with superscript annotations indicating baseline or reference methods; SOTA achieves highest scores across all datasets, while CLIP performs well in zero-shot settings.

caption: Table 15. Action recognition performance on 3 video datasets. Single model SOTA reported to best of knowledge. Note that linear $C L I P$ and linear NS ENet-L2 are trained and evaluated on a single frame subsampled version of each dataset and not directly comparable to prior work. On Kinetics-700, we report the ActivityNet competition metric which is the average of top-1 and top-5 performance. a(Kalfaoglu et al., 2020) b(Lu et al., 2020) c(Xie et al., 2020) d(Miech et al., 2020b) e(Carreira et al., 2019) f (Alayrac et al., 2020)
llm_description: This table compares performance across three video action recognition benchmarks (UCF101, K700, RareAct) for various models under three training regimes: Finetune, Linear, and ZS (Zero-Shot). It reports metrics including Top-1 accuracy, AVG, mWAP, and mWSAP, highlighting that CLIP achieves the highest scores in zero-shot settings (40.7 mWAP, 44.8 mWSAP) while R(2+1)D-BERT excels in fine-tuned UCF101 (98.7 Top-1).

llm_description: This table compares the top-1 accuracy (%) of four models—NS EfficientNet-L2, FixResNeXt101-32x48d V2, Linear Probe CLIP, and Zero-Shot CLIP—across nine diverse visual recognition benchmarks: IN Top-1, IN-V2 Top-1, IN-A Top-1, IN-R Top-1, ObjectNet Top-1, IN-Sketch Top-1, IN-Vid PM0/PM10, and YTBB PM0/PM10. It reveals that Zero-Shot CLIP achieves the highest scores across most benchmarks (notably 88.9% on IN-R and 95.3% on IN-Vid PM0), while NS EfficientNet-L2 performs best on IN Top-1 (88.3%) and IN-A Top-1 (84.9%).

caption: Table 17. Geolocalization performance on the IM2GPS test set. Metric is percent of images localized within a given radius. Models are ordered by average performance. a(Muller-Budack et al., 2018) b(Hongsuck Seo et al., 2018) c(Vo et al., 2017) $^ c$ (Weyand et al., 2016)
llm_description: This table compares the performance (likely in terms of accuracy or success rate) of five image retrieval models—ISNs, CPlaNet, CLIP, Deep-Ret+, and PlaNet—across five increasing distance scales (1km, 25km, 200km, 750km, and 2500km), showing how each model's effectiveness degrades as distance increases.

caption: Table 18. Common CLIP hyperparameters
llm_description: This table lists key hyperparameters and their values for a machine learning model, including batch size (32768), vocabulary size (49408), training epochs (32), maximum temperature (100.0), weight decay (0.2), warm-up iterations (2000), and Adam optimizer parameters (β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999 for ResNet and 0.98 for ViT, ε=10⁻⁸ for ResNet and 10⁻⁶ for ViT).
